question_title: Will Justice Kavanaugh be the author of the majority opinion in NRSC v. FEC?
summary: NRSC v. FEC is an undecided December-sitting opinion. Process of elimination (5 December authors already written; Hamm DIG'd; Roberts likely has Trump v. Slaughter; Sotomayor unlikely to author a likely-conservative campaign-finance majority) narrows authorship to essentially Kavanaugh vs Alito. Leading court-watcher Josh Blackman predicts Kavanaugh 'almost certainly,' but explicitly hedges that Alito is a live alternative. Expert authorship predictions are good but imperfect, and there is a small tail risk of delay past July 10 or a no-single-author disposition. Net tilt toward Kavanaugh from a coin flip.

factors.description: Process-of-elimination narrows NRSC to ~2 conservative candidates (Kavanaugh, Alito)
Blackman predicts Kavanaugh 'almost certainly' via assignment balancing and Alito being 'done'
Blackman's explicit hedge toward Alito; genuine residual uncertainty
Small risk of non-release by July 10 or per curiam/DIG/no single author

reasoning:
**Forecast: 0.61 (61%)**

NRSC v. FEC is an outstanding December 2025-sitting opinion. Process of elimination (Thomas, Kagan, Gorsuch, Barrett, and Jackson already authored December opinions; Hamm v. Smith was DIG'd; Roberts likely holds Trump v. Slaughter; Sotomayor is unlikely to author a likely-conservative campaign-finance majority) narrows authorship to essentially **Kavanaugh vs. Alito**.

Court-watcher Josh Blackman (Volokh) predicts Kavanaugh "almost certainly," but explicitly flags Alito as a live alternative. That expert lean justifies a tilt above a coin flip, tempered by the imperfect track record of authorship predictions and a small tail risk of delay past July 10 or a no-single-author disposition. Net estimate: **61%**.
sources_consulted: [Revisiting My Authorship Predictions And Making More Predictions](https://reason.com/volokh/2026/06/25/revisiting-my-authorship-predictions-and-making-more-predictions/)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if a majority opinion (the "Opinion of the Court") in National Republican Senatorial Committee v. Federal Election Commission (docket No. 24-621) is released between June 27, 2026 and July 10, 2026, and Justice Brett M. Kavanaugh is listed as its author. It resolves NO if such an opinion is released in that window authored by any other justice, or issued per curiam. The authoring justice is determined by the "J." (Justice) column for docket 24-621 on the Supreme Court's official 2025 Term slip-opinion index (https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/slipopinion/25), which lists "BK" for opinions authored by Justice Kavanaugh, cross-checked against the author shown on the first page of the released slip opinion.
fine_print: "Author of the majority opinion" means the justice who wrote the Opinion of the Court (the controlling majority opinion), as identified on the first page of the slip opinion and in the "J." column of the official slip-opinion index. If the Court resolves the case by a plurality opinion, the author of the plurality (lead) opinion announcing the judgment counts as the author for this question. A per curiam disposition, a dismissal of the writ as improvidently granted, or any disposition with no single identified opinion author resolves the question NO. If no Opinion of the Court in docket 24-621 has been released by 11:59 PM ET on July 9, 2026, the question resolves NO. If the slip-opinion index is unavailable, authorship will be determined from the released slip opinion PDF or, failing that, from reporting by credible sources (https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions).

factors.description: Process-of-elimination on December sitting narrows NRSC to ~2 conservative candidates (Kavanaugh, Alito); Sotomayor unlikely to author a likely-conservative majority, Roberts has Slaughter
Leading court-watcher (Blackman) predicts Kavanaugh 'almost certainly', based on assignment balancing and Alito being 'done' with 6 opinions
Blackman hedges: if Alito has NRSC it undermines his Hamm-DIG theory; genuine residual uncertainty between the two
Small risk opinion not released by July 10, or per curiam/DIG/no single author (major constitutional case, usually clear author)

input.anchor: ~50% two-way race between Kavanaugh and Alito after process-of-elimination
input.assessment: Two-way race Kavanaugh vs Alito, expert leans Kavanaugh strongly but with explicit hedge. Expert authorship predictions are good but imperfect. Timing/disposition risk is small for a major case expected by early July. Settling ~0.62.
input.tool_audit: web_search and fetch_url on Volokh worked well. supremecourt.gov slip index fetch returned generic template text (no live case data) — could not confirm whether already decided, but description says undecided as of late June.
input.process_reflection: Smooth for a definitional/assignment question. A tool to directly query the live SCOTUS slip-opinion index would have helped confirm non-release status.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
All four factors are grounded in the research trace. Verified:

- Factor 1 (two-way race via process of elimination): Trace confirms 5 December authors already written (Thomas, Kagan, Gorsuch, Barrett, Jackson), Hamm v. Smith DIG'd, outstanding December opinions = Slaughter (Roberts) and NRSC, with only Roberts/Alito/Sotomayor/Kavanaugh remaining. Logit +0.0 correctly treats this as a setup step.
- Factor 2 (Blackman "almost certainly" Kavanaugh): Directly quoted in trace, including the "Alito done with 6 opinions" reasoning. Logit +0.9 directionally correct.
- Factor 3 (Blackman's hedge on the Hamm-DIG theory): Trace confirms the exact reasoning that Alito having NRSC would undermine the theory. Logit -0.4 appropriately captures residual uncertainty — and importantly avoids double-counting by treating the hedge as a separate discount on the same expert source rather than re-asserting it.
- Factor 4 (timing/disposition risk): Reasonable small discount addressing the July 10 deadline and per curiam/DIG edge cases from the resolution criteria.

Resolution criteria are provided and well-engaged: factors map to the BK/J.-column author determination, the deadline, and the DIG/per curiam → NO conditions. No pre-publication resolution claim is made; Blackman's June 23/25 predictions are forward-looking, not resolutions, and the agent could not (and did not pretend to) confirm the case was decided.

Tool-audit narrative is consistent with ground truth (16 calls, 0 errors); the slip-index "template text" issue is a content limitation, not a tool failure, and is not overstated.

Probability: anchor ~50%, moving to 62% on a single moderately-strong, explicitly-hedged expert signal is an ~12pp departure — under the ~20pp threshold and evidence-driven. My independent estimate is also ~0.60–0.65: a targeted "almost certainly" expert prediction in a genuine two-way race, with the alternative (Alito) actively flagged, warrants a tilt but not high confidence. 62% is well-calibrated.

Counterargument is genuine (constructs the coherent Alito-alternative story with concrete falsifiers), and self-confidence of 0.70 appropriately reflects thin-but-real evidence plus unconfirmable resolution status. No hallucinations, double-counting, wrong-direction factors, or resolution misalignment found.
